children.name: Turdus
children.description: The true thrushes — medium-sized songbirds with spotted or plain underparts, a rich melodic song, and a taste for earthworms and berries. Turdus is one of the most successful and widespread bird genera on Earth. The Common Blackbird is the most familiar garden bird across much of Europe, renowned for its fluty, unhurried song delivered from rooftops and treetops at dusk — an almost universal soundtrack of the European spring.

children.name: Turdus migratorius
children.description: The American Robin is one of North America's most familiar birds, recognized by its brick-red breast and cheerful caroling song. It is a partial migrant that forms large winter flocks and is often the first songbird heard singing at dawn in spring.

children.name: Turdus grayi
children.description: The Clay-colored Thrush is the national bird of Costa Rica, appreciated for its rich, melodious song delivered throughout the rainy season. It is a plain, brownish thrush common in gardens, forest edges, and urban areas from Mexico to northern Colombia.

children.name: Turdus rufiventris
children.description: The Rufous-bellied Thrush is the national bird of Brazil, distinguished by its vivid orange-red belly contrasting with a grey-brown back. It is a common and vocal species found in forests, parks, and gardens across much of eastern and southern South America.

children.name: Turdus leucomelas
children.description: The Pale-breasted Thrush is a widespread South American thrush with a pale, lightly streaked breast and a bold yellow bill. It frequents open woodland, forest edges, and urban gardens from Venezuela south to Argentina.

children.name: Turdus atrogularis
children.description: The Black-throated Thrush is a striking Asian thrush in which the male bears a solid black throat and upper breast contrasting with white underparts. It breeds in Siberia and Central Asia and is a regular but rare vagrant to western Europe, prized by birders wherever it appears.

children.name: Turdus eunomus
children.description: The Dusky Thrush is a boldly patterned East Asian thrush with heavy black scaling on white underparts and warm rufous in the wings. It breeds in north-eastern Siberia and migrates in large flocks to winter in China, Japan, and South-East Asia.

children.name: Turdus kessleri
children.description: Kessler's Thrush is a large, striking thrush of high-altitude scrub and alpine meadows on the Tibetan Plateau, named after the Russian zoologist Johann Friedrich Kessler. Males are boldly patterned in black, white, and chestnut; it is scarce and poorly studied owing to its remote range.

children.name: Turdus rubrocanus
children.description: The Chestnut Thrush is a large, richly coloured thrush of Himalayan and Chinese mountain forests, with deep chestnut upperparts contrasting with a dark grey head and streaked throat. It breeds in coniferous and mixed forest at high elevation, descending to wooded valleys in winter.

children.name: Turdus boulboul
children.description: The Grey-winged Blackbird is an elegant Himalayan thrush in which the male is jet black with a bold pale grey wing panel and a bright orange-yellow bill. It inhabits oak and rhododendron forests along the Himalayas from Pakistan to south-west China, and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus unicolor
children.description: Tickell's Thrush is a plain, pale-grey Asian thrush named after the British officer and naturalist Samuel Tickell, found in forest and scrub across the Himalayas and Indian subcontinent. Its unassuming plumage belies a pleasing, melodic song; it is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.

children.name: Turdus dissimilis
children.description: The Black-breasted Thrush is a distinctive Himalayan and South-East Asian thrush in which the male has a black head, throat, and breast contrasting sharply with white and rufous underparts. It inhabits dense forest undergrowth in montane areas from north-east India to southern China.

children.name: Turdus feae
children.description: Fea's Thrush is a rare and poorly known thrush named after the Italian explorer and naturalist Leonardo Fea, breeding only in a restricted area of the mountains of Myanmar and adjacent south-west China. It is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to its small range and ongoing habitat loss.

children.name: Turdus albicollis
children.description: The White-necked Thrush is a wide-ranging South American forest thrush distinguished by its clean white throat and upper breast contrasting with dark brown upperparts. It is found from Colombia and Venezuela south to northern Argentina, favouring the interior of humid forest.

children.name: Turdus amaurochalinus
children.description: The Creamy-bellied Thrush is a common open-country and woodland thrush of southern South America, with a pale creamy-white belly and lightly streaked throat. It is found from Bolivia and Brazil south to Argentina and Chile, and adapts readily to parks and urban gardens.

children.name: Turdus chiguanco
children.description: The Chiguanco Thrush is a large, uniformly dark thrush of Andean valleys and high-altitude towns from Ecuador and Peru south to north-western Argentina. Its deep, rolling song echoes through Andean cities at dawn and dusk, where it is one of the most familiar urban birds.

children.name: Turdus serranus
children.description: The Glossy-black Thrush is a sleek, entirely black thrush of Andean cloud forests, with a bright orange-yellow bill and eye-ring in the male. It ranges from Venezuela and Colombia south to Bolivia and is typically found in the upper understorey of humid montane forest.

children.name: Turdus nigriceps
children.description: The Andean Slaty Thrush is a subtly plumaged thrush of Andean forests and adjacent lowlands in Peru, Bolivia, and north-west Argentina, with slaty-grey upperparts and a pale, streaked throat. It inhabits forest interiors and edges at mid to high elevations, and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus fuscater
children.description: The Great Thrush is the largest South American thrush, a bulky, dark sooty-brown bird with a vivid orange bill found commonly in Andean cloud forests, parks, and gardens from Venezuela to Bolivia. It produces a remarkably rich and varied song and is one of the most conspicuous birds of high Andean cities.

children.name: Turdus olivaceus
children.description: The Olive Thrush is the most widespread thrush of sub-Saharan Africa, a robust, dark-backed species with an orange-yellow bill and orange-buff flanks common in forest margins, gardens, and highland parks. It is the familiar garden thrush of South Africa and East Africa and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus abyssinicus
children.description: The African Thrush is a familiar, orange-billed thrush widespread across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal east to Ethiopia and south to Angola, common in forest edge, gallery forest, and large gardens. Its loud, varied song is a characteristic sound of African woodlands; it is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus pelios
children.description: The West African Thrush is a plain, grey-brown thrush with a yellowish bill found across the forests and woodland edges of West and Central Africa, from Senegal and Guinea east to Uganda. It is a versatile species tolerating secondary forest and is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.

children.name: Turdus helleri
children.description: The Taita Thrush is a critically endangered thrush named after the American zoologist Edmund Heller, restricted to tiny fragments of montane forest in the Taita Hills of south-east Kenya. Its total population numbers in the hundreds, and it is one of the most threatened birds in Africa.

children.name: Turdus menachensis
children.description: The Yemen Thrush is a distinctive, boldly marked thrush endemic to the montane forests and juniper woodlands of south-west Saudi Arabia and Yemen. Its restricted range and ongoing deforestation have raised concerns, and it is currently listed as Near Threatened by the IUCN.

children.name: Turdus plumbeus
children.description: The Red-legged Thrush is a boldly patterned Caribbean thrush with striking red legs, eye-ring, and bill base, found across the Greater Antilles and the northern Bahamas. It inhabits forest, woodland, and gardens on its island homes and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus jamaicensis
children.description: The White-eyed Thrush is a Jamaican endemic with a distinctive white iris that becomes more prominent with age. It inhabits forest and woodland across Jamaica, where it is a frugivore and insectivore of the mid and upper storey; it is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus fumigatus
children.description: The Cocoa Thrush is a warm, richly brown thrush of forest interior and edge found from Trinidad and the Lesser Antilles south through Venezuela, Guyana, and the Amazon basin. It is named for its deep cocoa-brown plumage and is an accomplished songster; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus nudigenis
children.description: The Bare-eyed Thrush is a pale, sandy-brown thrush of open woodland and scrub distinguished by its large, conspicuous yellow orbital ring. It ranges from Tobago and the Lesser Antilles through Venezuela and Colombia and has established introduced populations in Florida; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Turdus poliocephalus
children.description: The Island Thrush is one of the most polytypic birds in the world, with over 50 recognised subspecies ranging across Pacific and Indian Ocean islands from the Andamans to Samoa and Fiji. Plumage varies enormously between island populations, from all-black males to those resembling Eurasian Blackbirds, offering a remarkable example of insular variation.

children.name: Turdus swalesi
children.description: The La Selle Thrush is a rare and little-known Caribbean thrush named after the American ornithologist Lester Swales, restricted to montane forests in the La Selle and La Hotte massifs of Haiti and the Sierra de Baoruco of the Dominican Republic. It is listed as Endangered by the IUCN due to the continuing destruction of Hispaniolan cloud forest.

children.name: Geokichla
children.description: Ground thrushes with bold spotted or scaled underparts, typically inhabitants of dense forest undergrowth. The Siberian Thrush is a spectacular long-distance migrant that breeds in the taiga of Siberia and winters in Southeast Asia, yet is so secretive on its breeding grounds that the nest was not described until the 20th century.

children.name: Geokichla piaggiae
children.description: The Abyssinian Ground Thrush is a secretive forest-floor thrush named after the Italian explorer Carlo Piaggia, found in montane forests of East Africa from Ethiopia to Uganda. It is classified as Least Concern but remains poorly studied due to its retiring habits in dense undergrowth.

children.name: Geokichla spiloptera
children.description: The Spot-winged Thrush is a handsome, boldly spotted forest thrush endemic to Sri Lanka, with striking black-and-white wing spots and a richly scaled orange-buff and black pattern below. It inhabits primary and mature secondary forest and is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN owing to deforestation.

children.name: Geokichla interpres
children.description: The Chestnut-capped Thrush is a small, secretive forest-floor thrush with a bold chestnut crown contrasting with white underparts and black scaling, found from Myanmar and Thailand south through the Malay Peninsula to Borneo. It forages among leaf litter in the understorey of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Geokichla lunulata
children.description: The Bassian Thrush is a richly scaled Australian thrush of moist eucalyptus forest and rainforest, with intricate brown and black crescent-shaped markings across its underparts. It feeds quietly on the forest floor and is named for the Bass Strait region where it was first collected; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Geokichla heinei
children.description: The Russet-tailed Thrush is an Australian forest thrush named after the German ornithologist Ferdinand Heine, distinguished from the Bassian Thrush by its plain rufous-brown upperparts and less conspicuous scaling below. It inhabits subtropical rainforest in coastal Queensland and New South Wales; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Geokichla gurneyi
children.description: The Orange Ground Thrush is a handsome African forest-floor thrush named after the British ornithologist John Henry Gurney, with a vivid orange face and underparts contrasting with olive-brown upperparts. It inhabits montane forest from South Africa north to Tanzania and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Zoothera
children.description: Large, heavily scaled thrushes of forests and woodland. White's Thrush is the largest thrush to occur in Europe, a rare but spectacular vagrant from Siberia and East Asia with a bold crescent-scaled pattern unique among European birds. It forages quietly on the forest floor, flicking leaves aside with its bill in search of earthworms and beetles.

children.name: Zoothera dauma
children.description: The Scaly Thrush is a large, boldly patterned thrush covered in black-edged golden-buff scales across its upperparts and underparts. It inhabits dense montane forests from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia and is considered Least Concern by the IUCN.

children.name: Zoothera andromedae
children.description: The Sunda Thrush is a boldly scaled forest thrush of the mountain forests of the Greater Sunda Islands — Sumatra, Java, and Borneo — closely related to the Scaly Thrush but now treated as a separate species. It inhabits the interior of montane rainforest and is rarely seen due to its secretive habits.

children.name: Zoothera marginata
children.description: The Long-billed Thrush is a dusky brown, long-billed thrush of dense Himalayan undergrowth from north-east India and Nepal east to south-west China. Its notably long bill is an adaptation for probing deep into leaf litter and soil, and its retiring habits make it seldom observed despite a wide range.

children.name: Zoothera monticola
children.description: The Long-tailed Thrush is a large, dark brown thrush with a prominently long tail found in dense, moist forest undergrowth in the eastern Himalayas and adjacent hills of Myanmar and south-west China. It creeps along the ground like a rail and is extremely difficult to observe.

children.name: Zoothera crossleyi
children.description: Crossley's Ground Thrush is an African forest-floor thrush named after John Crossley, a collector for the ornithologist Jules Verreaux, found in montane forests of Cameroon, eastern DRC, Rwanda, and western Uganda. It is secretive and poorly known, lurking in the dense undergrowth of high-altitude forest.

children.name: Catharus
children.description: Spotted thrushes of North and Central America, renowned for some of the most beautiful songs of any bird. The Hermit Thrush and Swainson's Thrush produce haunting, flute-like phrases that carry far through forest understory. Several species are long-distance migrants, wintering in the tropics.

children.name: Catharus guttatus
children.description: The Hermit Thrush is the only spotted Catharus thrush to winter in North America, and is widely regarded as producing one of the finest bird songs in the world — a series of ethereal, ascending flute-like spirals. It is the state bird of Vermont and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Catharus ustulatus
children.description: Swainson's Thrush is a long-distance migrant named after the English ornithologist William Swainson, breeding in boreal forests across North America and wintering in South America. Its spiraling, upward-winding song is a hallmark of northern forest evenings in spring and summer.

children.name: Catharus fuscescens
children.description: The Veery is a warm russet-brown spotted thrush known for its cascading, downward-spiraling song that sounds like a descending vortex. It breeds in moist deciduous forests of North America and undertakes one of the longest migrations of any thrush, wintering in central Brazil.

children.name: Catharus minimus
children.description: The Grey-cheeked Thrush is a long-distance boreal migrant with a pale grey face lacking an obvious eye-ring, breeding in spruce-fir forests from Siberia across Alaska and Canada. Its thin, nasal song is a common sound of migration nights; it winters in the Amazon basin and Andean foothills.

children.name: Catharus bicknelli
children.description: Bicknell's Thrush is a small, secretive Catharus thrush named after the American naturalist Eugene Pintard Bicknell, breeding only in dense montane fir forests on the highest peaks of north-eastern North America and wintering in the Greater Antilles. It is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN owing to its highly restricted breeding habitat.

children.name: Catharus aurantiirostris
children.description: The Orange-billed Nightingale-Thrush is a retiring forest-understorey thrush of montane cloud forests and shaded ravines from Mexico to northern Venezuela and Colombia, identified by its vivid orange bill, legs, and eye-ring. Despite its retiring habits, its rich, melodious song is frequently heard in the highland forests it inhabits.

children.name: Catharus dryas
children.description: The Spotted Nightingale-Thrush is a boldly spotted, orange-billed thrush of dense undergrowth in humid montane forest from southern Mexico to north-western South America. It is skulking and difficult to observe, but its loud, liquid song is one of the finest of any Middle American forest bird.

children.name: Hylocichla
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the Wood Thrush, one of the most celebrated songbirds of eastern North America. Its rich, flute-like song — a complex series of phrases delivered in the dappled shade of deciduous forest — has inspired poets and naturalists for centuries. Populations have declined sharply due to forest fragmentation and cowbird parasitism.

children.name: Hylocichla mustelina
children.description: The Wood Thrush is a plump, richly spotted thrush of eastern North American deciduous forest celebrated for its ethereal, flute-like song of interwoven harmonics. It winters in Central American lowland forest, and populations have declined significantly due to deforestation and brood parasitism; it is listed as Near Threatened by the IUCN.

children.name: Sialia
children.description: Bluebirds — small thrushes of North and Central America with brilliant blue or blue-and-chestnut plumage. Males are among the most colorful of all North American songbirds. They nest in cavities and have benefited from nest box programs that reversed dramatic population declines caused by competition from introduced species.

children.name: Sialia sialis
children.description: The Eastern Bluebird is a beloved North American thrush with vivid blue upperparts and a rich chestnut-red breast, often called a harbinger of spring. It is the state bird of Missouri and New York and has rebounded strongly thanks to continent-wide nest box programs; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sialia currucoides
children.description: The Mountain Bluebird is an ethereal, sky-blue thrush of western North American mountain meadows, lacking the chestnut breast of its relatives. Males hover like kestrels over open ground to detect insects, and the species is the state bird of Idaho and Nevada; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sialia mexicana
children.description: The Western Bluebird is a stunning small thrush of open pine and oak woodlands in western North America and Mexico, with deep blue upperparts, a blue throat, and a rich chestnut breast patch. It nests in old woodpecker holes or nest boxes and is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.

children.name: Ixoreus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the Varied Thrush, a striking Pacific coast thrush with bold orange and black patterning unlike any other North American thrush. It inhabits dense, humid old-growth coniferous forests and is known for its eerie, single-note buzzing whistle.

children.name: Ixoreus naevius
children.description: The Varied Thrush is a stunning Pacific coast thrush with orange eyebrow stripes, wingbars, and breast contrasting with blue-grey upperparts and a dark breast-band. It breeds in old-growth coniferous rainforests from Alaska to California, wintering at lower elevations; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Myadestes
children.description: Solitaires — slender, long-tailed thrushes of montane forests in the Americas and Hawaii, known for extraordinarily melodious songs. Several Hawaiian species have gone extinct due to introduced predators and disease, making conservation of surviving island species a priority.

children.name: Myadestes townsendi
children.description: Townsend's Solitaire is a slender, grey thrush named after the American naturalist John Kirk Townsend, found in montane coniferous forests of western North America. It is a territorial winter frugivore that defends juniper berry patches, and is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.

children.name: Myadestes obscurus
children.description: The Omao is a plain, dark brown solitaire endemic to the forests of the Big Island of Hawaii, producing a rich and complex song. It is the only native thrush still relatively common in Hawaii, having avoided the fate of its now-extinct relatives; it is listed as Vulnerable due to ongoing habitat loss and avian diseases.

children.name: Myadestes elisabeth
children.description: The Cuban Solitaire is a slender grey thrush named in honour of Princess Elisabeth of France, endemic to the mountain pine and cloud forests of Cuba and the Isle of Youth. Its extraordinary, complex song — one of the finest in the Caribbean — rings out from dense montane vegetation; it is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Myadestes genibarbis
children.description: The Rufous-throated Solitaire is a distinctive slender thrush of Lesser Antilles and Caribbean montane forests, with a warm rufous throat that sets it apart from its plain-faced relatives. Its haunting, pure whistled song is considered one of the most beautiful sounds of the Caribbean highlands; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Myadestes melanops
children.description: The Black-faced Solitaire is a stunning Central American solitaire with a velvety black face mask contrasting with blue-grey plumage and an orange bill and legs. Endemic to the cloud forests of Costa Rica and western Panama, it is a prized cage bird in its native range; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Myadestes ralloides
children.description: The Andean Solitaire is a slender, grey thrush of humid Andean cloud forest from Venezuela and Colombia south to Bolivia, with a slight orange tinge on the bill. Its extraordinary song consists of rich, varied flute-like phrases and is considered one of the finest of all Andean forest birds; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Platycichla
children.description: A small South American genus of thrushes closely related to Turdus but with a flatter bill profile. The Yellow-legged Thrush of the tepui highlands is a striking, sexually dimorphic species in which the male is entirely glossy black with yellow legs and eye-ring.

children.name: Platycichla flavipes
children.description: The Yellow-legged Thrush is a handsome forest thrush in which the male is glossy blue-black with yellow legs, bill, and eye-ring; females are brown above and streaked below. It inhabits humid forest and forest edge from Trinidad and Venezuela south through the Guianas and eastern Brazil; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Platycichla leucops
children.description: The Pale-eyed Thrush is a sleek, all-black thrush with a strikingly white or cream iris found in humid forest on Andean slopes and tepuis from Colombia and Venezuela south to Peru. The pale eye gives it an arresting appearance; it inhabits primary forest interior and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Psophocichla
children.description: A monotypic African genus containing the Groundscraper Thrush, a large, boldly spotted thrush of open dry savanna that forages almost exclusively on bare ground. Unlike the secretive forest-floor habits of most thrushes, it is conspicuous and confident in open habitats from southern to east-central Africa.

children.name: Psophocichla litsitsirupa
children.description: The Groundscraper Thrush is a large, distinctive African thrush with bold black spots on white underparts and an upright stance, found in open savanna, dry woodland, and airstrips from Ethiopia south to South Africa. It feeds almost entirely on the ground, walking deliberately in the open, and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Chlamydochaera
children.description: A monotypic Bornean genus containing the Fruithunter, an unusual Turdidae member that superficially resembles a miniature pitta with its bold patterning. It inhabits the interior of montane rainforest on Borneo, where it feeds on small fruits and arthropods on or near the forest floor.

children.name: Chlamydochaera jefferyi
children.description: The Fruithunter is a distinctive Bornean montane thrush named after the colonial official Charles Brooke Jefferys, with a black head, white underparts, and a chestnut-orange back creating a pitta-like appearance. It is restricted to montane forests above 900 m on Borneo and is listed as Near Threatened owing to habitat loss.

children.name: Geomalia
children.description: A monotypic genus from the island of Sulawesi, Indonesia, containing the Sulawesi Thrush — a large, ground-haunting thrush of montane forest interior that is poorly known due to the remoteness and difficulty of its mountain habitat. It was long thought to be an Old World flycatcher before being placed in Turdidae.

children.name: Geomalia heinrichi
children.description: The Sulawesi Thrush is a large, dark, long-legged thrush endemic to the montane forests of Sulawesi, Indonesia, named after the German ornithologist and collector Gerd Heinrich who described it. It inhabits forest floor and undergrowth above 1,500 m and its biology remains poorly documented; it is classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN.
